behaviors.description: Cron parser rejects invalid expressions with descriptive error messages
behaviors.justification: Input validation is a local function boundary with no I/O. The parser takes a string, checks it against syntax and range rules (month 1-12, day 1-31, valid field count, numeric values), and returns either a parsed object or an error. All failure modes (bad month, bad day, malformed syntax) are caught by pure parsing logic testable with fixed inputs. No system libraries, no mocking needed, no external dependencies.
behaviors.test_description: Parametrized tests providing invalid cron strings: month 13, day 32, day 0, invalid field separators, extra/missing fields, non-numeric values. Assert each rejects with a specific error message. Also test boundary cases like month 1, day 1, day 31 that should pass.

behaviors.description: Next-run-time calculator returns correct timestamps for standard cron including month-end and nearest-weekday edge cases
behaviors.justification: The calculator is a pure function: given (cron_expr, start_datetime), return next N run times as timestamps. Calendar math is deterministic and testable with fixed inputs. Edge cases like Feb 29, month lengths, and nearest-weekday logic are all computable without real infrastructure. The only question is whether the implementation delegates to a platform-specific library with bugs, but that would be a library bug, not a calculator bug. A unit test with fixed start times and expected outputs catches all calculator logic errors.
behaviors.test_description: Parametrized tests for diverse cron expressions: hourly, daily, monthly, complex ranges. Include edge cases: February 29 (leap year), month transitions (Jan 31 → Feb 28/29), last day of month, nearest weekday. For each, fix a start datetime and assert the next N run times match expected timestamps. Test boundary ordering (ascending times).

behaviors.description: Overlap detector identifies schedules firing within minimum gap and reports conflicting time windows
behaviors.justification: The detector is a comparison and window-merging function: given two schedules' run times and a gap threshold, identify conflicts. This is pure logic — no mocking hides the comparison bug. You can unit-test by providing fixed run-time lists (either computed once and reused, or mocked lists) and asserting the detector correctly identifies overlaps within the gap and correctly excludes non-overlaps. The gap threshold application and window reporting are both local logic.
behaviors.test_description: Test detector with pre-computed or mocked run-time lists: two schedules with overlapping times (within gap), two with non-overlapping times (gap respected), edge cases like run times exactly at the gap boundary. Assert correct identification of conflicts and empty result for non-conflicts. Test window reporting (start, end, duration of conflict).

behaviors.description: Timezone-aware scheduling handles DST transitions (spring-forward gap and fall-back ambiguity)
behaviors.justification: DST behavior depends on the system's timezone library and IANA data. While modern systems use deterministic IANA timezone data, the implementation must correctly call the library functions, pass the right timezone strings, and interpret the library's output. A unit test mocking datetime/timezone calls could miss bugs where the real library behaves unexpectedly (e.g., returning ambiguous times without a fold parameter, or raising exceptions for non-existent times). An integration test using real library calls (in-process, no subprocess) catches these real-world failures. The test must use known DST dates (e.g., US Eastern 2023-03-12 spring forward, 2023-11-05 fall back) to assert correctness.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test using real timezone library and fixed DST dates. For spring forward (2:00 AM → 3:00 AM), schedule a job for 2:30 AM and verify the next run is 3:30 AM (or skipped, depending on policy). For fall back (2:00 AM occurs twice), verify the job fires once or twice per policy. Assert correct handling of ambiguous and non-existent times.

behaviors.description: Engine determines skip-or-backfill action for missed runs based on configured policy
behaviors.justification: The missed-run policy is a decision function: given current_time, last_run, next_run, and policy, decide action (skip, backfill-once, backfill-all, etc.). This is pure logic with no I/O. You can unit-test by mocking the time source (e.g., fix current_time to a specific datetime after a scheduled run) and asserting the engine computes the correct action. The logic doesn't depend on real system state — it compares times and applies rules. Mocking doesn't hide failures in the policy logic itself (only failures in the time-comparison logic, which are part of the unit test).
behaviors.test_description: Parametrized tests for each policy: current_time set 10 minutes after a scheduled run. Policy 'skip' → assert no action. Policy 'backfill-once' → assert single immediate execution. Policy 'backfill-all' → assert all missed runs queued for immediate execution. Also test edge cases: current_time exactly at run time (no miss), current_time just before run time (no miss yet). Use mocked datetime to control the test timeline.
